text: “‘Despair not. To be without friends is indeed a hard lot; yet the hearts of men, when not warped by some manifest regard to their own advantage, are filled with brotherly kindness and charity. Rest, therefore, upon your hopes; and if these friends be good and amiable, despair not.’
source_text: “‘Do not despair. To be friendless is indeed to be unfortunate, but the hearts of men, when unprejudiced by any obvious self-interest, are full of brotherly love and charity. Rely, therefore, on your hopes; and if these friends are good and amiable, do not despair.’

text: Great God! what a scene has but now passed before me! I am still giddy with the recollection of it. I scarce know whether I shall have the strength to recount it; yet the tale I have set down would be left unfinished without this last and marvellous catastrophe.

text:
SEMICHORUS 1 [SONG WITHIN]:
Hark! hark! he comes apace,
A most fell discord in his pace.
Reeling, muse-less, clumsy, crying,
Far adown his stony dwelling;
Let us, by some comic charm,
School the still unschoolable from harm.
By all means must he be blinded,
If my counsel be but minded.
source_text:
SEMICHORUS 1 [SONG WITHIN]:
Listen! listen! he is coming,
A most hideous discord humming.
Drunken, museless, awkward, yelling,
Far along his rocky dwelling;
Let us with some comic spell
Teach the yet unteachable.
By all means he must be blinded,
If my counsel be but minded.

text:
My footfall frightens the wood-drake and wood-duck on my far and  
day-long ramble,  
They lift together, they slowly wheel about.
source_text:
My tread scares the wood-drake and wood-duck on my distant and
day-long ramble,

text:
Nor whither went, nor wherefore came  
The second time to me,  
Nor surely what it verily was,  
Have I the skill to say.
source_text:
Nor where it went, nor why it came
The second time to me,

text:
Word above all, as lovely as the heavens,
Lovely that war and all its works of slaughter must in time be
utterly forgotten,
That the hands of the sisters Death and Night, without cease, with gentle
soothing, wash again, and yet again, this steadfast world;
For my foe is dead, a man as godlike as myself is dead,
I gaze where he lies pallid and still in the coffin—I draw nigh,
I bend and lightly touch with my lips the white face in the coffin.
source_text:
Word over all, beautiful as the sky,
Beautiful that war and all its deeds of carnage must in time be
utterly lost,
That the hands of the sisters Death and Night incessantly softly
wash again, and ever again, this solid world;
For my enemy is dead, a man divine as myself is dead,
I look where he lies white-faced and still in the coffin--I draw near,
Bend down and touch lightly with my lips the white face in the coffin.

text:
And who now feels discord or grief?
Love is the universe to-day—
These are the thralls of shadowy morrow,
That dim Life’s labyrinthine way.
source_text:
And who feels discord now or sorrow?
Love is the universe to-day—
These are the slaves of dim to-morrow,
Darkening Life’s labyrinthine way.

text:
I know I hold the best of time and space, and was never measured and
never shall be measured.
source_text:
I know I have the best of time and space, and was never measured and
never will be measured.

text:
While we rehearse our boundless wealth, 'tis for thee, dear Mother,  
We hold it all, each part, to-day, inseparable in thee;  
Think not our song, our pageant, merely for base wares or gold—  
It is for thee, the soul within thee, electric, spiritual!  
Our farms, our arts, our harvests, we possess in thee! cities and States in thee!  
Our freedom all in thee! our very lives in thee!
source_text:
While we rehearse our measureless wealth, it is for thee, dear Mother,
We own it all and several to-day indissoluble in thee;
Think not our chant, our show, merely for products gross or lucre--
it is for thee, the soul in thee, electric, spiritual!
Our farms, inventions, crops, we own in thee! cities and States in thee!
Our freedom all in thee! our very lives in thee!

text:
And as we sailed, a strange and awful tale  
That Woman told, like some mysterious dream  
That makes the slumberer’s cheek with wonder pale!  
’Twas midnight, and around, a shoreless stream,  
The wide sea rolled, when that majestic theme  
Enshrined within her heart found utterance, and she bent  
Her looks on mine; those eyes, a kindling beam  
Of love divine, into my spirit sent,  
And ere her lips could move, made the air eloquent.
source_text:
And as we sailed, a strange and awful tale
That Woman told, like such mysterious dream
As makes the slumberer’s cheek with wonder pale!
’Twas midnight, and around, a shoreless stream,
Wide ocean rolled, when that majestic theme
Shrined in her heart found utterance, and she bent
Her looks on mine; those eyes a kindling beam
Of love divine into my spirit sent,
And ere her lips could move, made the air eloquent.

text:
Who made the heart, ’tis He alone  
Can surely search and prove us;  
He knows each chord, its varied tone,  
Each spring, its several bias:  
Then at the balance let us cease,  
Nor seek to set it right;  
What’s done we may in part surmise,  
But know not what withstood it.
source_text:
Who made the heart, ’tis He alone
Decidedly can try us;
He knows each chord, its various tone,
Each spring, its various bias:
Then at the balance let’s be mute,
We never can adjust it;
What’s done we partly may compute,
But know not what’s resisted.

text:
And she beheld princes couched beneath the sheen
Of sunlike jewels; and round each temple-court
In dormitories set, row after row,
She saw the priests asleep—all of one sort—
For all had been instructed thus, to be so.—
The peasants in their huts, and in the port
The sailors she beheld, rocked upon the waves,
And the dead hushed within their dreamless graves.

text:
IONE:  
Ah, me! dear sister,  
The tide of sound hath ebbed away from us,  
And thou dost feign to rise from out its wave,  
Because thy words fall like the lucid, tender dew  
Shaken from a bathing wood-nymph’s limbs and hair.
source_text:
IONE:
Ah me! sweet sister,
The stream of sound has ebbed away from us,
And you pretend to rise out of its wave,
Because your words fall like the clear, soft dew
Shaken from a bathing wood-nymph’s limbs and hair.

text:

Dropped from the stalks where erst they used to gleam;

As winds had done with those in upper air.

text:
Is this then a touch? quivering me into a newer self,
Flame and ether hurrying up through my veins,
False and traitorous end of me reaching and thronging to aid them,
My flesh and blood casting out lightning to smite what is scarcely
other than myself,
On every side lewd tempters hardening my limbs,
Milking the udder of my heart for its withheld drop,
Using me wantonly, granting no refusal,
Robbing me of my choicest as though for some design,
Unbuttoning my garments, gripping me by the naked waist,
Beguiling my bewilderment with the quiet of sunlight and fieldward pastures,
Shamelessly slipping the fellow-senses aside,
They were bribed to exchange themselves for touch and to crop at my borders,
No pity, no heed for my ebbing strength or my wrath,
Calling the rest of the herd to come and feast on them awhile,
Then all together to stand on a headland and harry me.
source_text:
Is this then a touch? quivering me to a new identity,
Flames and ether making a rush for my veins,
Treacherous tip of me reaching and crowding to help them,
My flesh and blood playing out lightning to strike what is hardly
different from myself,
On all sides prurient provokers stiffening my limbs,
Straining the udder of my heart for its withheld drip,
Behaving licentious toward me, taking no denial,
Depriving me of my best as for a purpose,
Unbuttoning my clothes, holding me by the bare waist,
Deluding my confusion with the calm of the sunlight and pasture-fields,
Immodestly sliding the fellow-senses away,
They bribed to swap off with touch and go and graze at the edges of me,
No consideration, no regard for my draining strength or my anger,
Fetching the rest of the herd around to enjoy them a while,
Then all uniting to stand on a headland and worry me.

text:
Laws for creations,  
For puissant artists and leaders, for new broods of teachers and  
perfect literati for America,  
For noble savants and musicians yet to come.

compact truth of the world,  
There shall be no theme too marked--all works shall show

text:
I tasted wheat, and loathed the chaff,
And thanked the generous friend;
Wisdom looks comelier when beheld
From far, than near at hand.
source_text:
I tasted wheat, -- and hated chaff,
And thanked the ample friend;
Wisdom is more becoming viewed
At distance than at hand.

text:
In answer then, the sea,  
Lingering not, hastening not,  
Whisper’d unto me through the night, and very clearly ere the dawn,  
Lisp’d to me the low and delectable word death,  
And once again death, death, death, death  
Hissing in melody, neither as the bird nor as my awaken’d child’s heart,  
But drawing close as secretly for me, whispering at my feet,  
Creeping thence unerringly up to mine ears and bathing me softly over all,  
Death, death, death, death, death.
source_text:
Whereto answering, the sea,
Delaying not, hurrying not,
Whisper’d me through the night, and very plainly before daybreak,
Lisp’d to me the low and delicious word death,
And again death, death, death, death
Hissing melodious, neither like the bird nor like my arous’d child’s heart,
But edging near as privately for me rustling at my feet,
Creeping thence steadily up to my ears and laving me softly all over,
Death, death, death, death, death.

text:
There is a triumph in the finer mind  
When truth, long put to shame,  
Moves forward calm toward her supreme,  
Her God her only name.
source_text:
There 's triumph of the finer mind
When truth, affronted long,
Advances calm to her supreme,
Her God her only throng.

text:
Reckoning before thee, O soul, when thou, the season won,  
The seas all pass’d, the headlands weather’d, the voyage done,  
Encompass’d, thou dost face and meet God, yielding, the end attain’d,  
As, full of friendship, love made perfect, the Elder Brother found,  
The Younger melts in tenderness within his arms.
source_text:
Reckoning ahead O soul, when thou, the time achiev’d,
The seas all cross’d, weather’d the capes, the voyage done,
Surrounded, copest, frontest God, yieldest, the aim attain’d,
As fill’d with friendship, love complete, the Elder Brother found,
The Younger melts in fondness in his arms.

text:
Far from love the Heavenly Father
Guides the chosen child;
Oftener through a land of briers
Than the meadow mild,
source_text:

Leads the chosen child;

text:
No bone had he to hold him,
His utterance was the gush
Of countless humming-birds at once
From an upper bush.
source_text:
No bone had he to bind him,
His speech was like the push
Of numerous humming-birds at once
From a superior bush.

text:
As one by one the stately players vanish,
From that vast drama on history’s endless stage,
That glaring, partisan scene of war and peace—of old and new in conflict,
Wrought through rage, through fear, through midnight dread, and many a weary pause;
All gone—and since, in numberless graves receding, softening,
Conqueror’s and vanquish’d—Lincoln’s and Lee’s—now thou with them,
Man of the mighty days—and match’d to the days!
Thou from the prairies!—tangled and many-vein’d and hard hath been thy lot,
To wonder has it been performed!
source_text:
As one by one withdraw the lofty actors,
From that great play on history’s stage eterne,
That lurid, partial act of war and peace--of old and new contending,
Fought out through wrath, fears, dark dismays, and many a long suspense;
All past--and since, in countless graves receding, mellowing,
Victor’s and vanquish’d--Lincoln’s and Lee’s--now thou with them,
Man of the mighty days--and equal to the days!
Thou from the prairies!--tangled and many-vein’d and hard has been thy part,
To admiration has it been enacted!

text: My days were passed in close observance, that I might more swiftly master the tongue; and I may boast I advanced more rapidly than the Arabian, who understood but little and spake in broken accents, whilst I comprehended and could counterfeit almost every word that was uttered.
source_text: “My days were spent in close attention, that I might more speedily master the language; and I may boast that I improved more rapidly than the Arabian, who understood very little and conversed in broken accents, whilst I comprehended and could imitate almost every word that was spoken.

text:
What though, like commoners of air,  
We stray away, we know not where,  
To house or hall alight;  
Yet nature’s graces, hills and woods,  
The sweeping vales, the foaming floods,  
Are free to all by right.  
In days when daisies deck the ground,  
And blackbirds whistle clear,  
With honest joy our hearts shall bound  
To greet the coming year:  
On braes whene’er we please, then,  
We’ll sit and soothe a tune;  
Syne rhyme till’t, we’ll time till’t,  
And sing it when we’re done.
source_text:
What tho’, like commoners of air,
We wander out, we know not where,
But either house or hal’,
Yet nature’s charms, the hills and woods,
The sweeping vales, and foaming floods,
Are free alike to all.
In days when daisies deck the ground,
And blackbirds whistle clear,
With honest joy our hearts will bound,
To see the coming year:
On braes when we please, then,
We’ll sit an’ sowth a tune;
Syne rhyme till’t we’ll time till’t,
An’ sing’t when we hae done.

text:
Nothing is sinful to us beyond ourselves,  
Whatsoe'er appears, whatsoe'er appears not, we are fair or  
sinful within ourselves alone.
source_text:
Nothing is sinful to us outside of ourselves,
Whatever appears, whatever does not appear, we are beautiful or
sinful in ourselves only.

text:
I know a place where summer strives  
With frost so well rehearsed,  
She each year leads her daisies back,  
And briefly writes, “Lost.”
source_text:
I know a place where summer strives
With such a practised frost,
She each year leads her daisies back,
Recording briefly, "Lost."

text:
PROMETHEUS:
There are twin ills:
To speak, and to behold; grant me one.
There were the names, great Nature’s hallowed watchwords, they
Were lifted high in shining blazonry;
The nations thronged around, and cried aloud,
With one accord, Truth, liberty, and love!
Then, sudden, from the heavens fell fierce confusion
Among them: strife, deceit, and fear arose:
And tyrants rushed in, and divided the spoil.
Such was the shadow of the truth I saw.
source_text:
PROMETHEUS:
There are two woes:
To speak, and to behold; thou spare me one.
Names are there, Nature’s sacred watchwords, they
Were borne aloft in bright emblazonry;
The nations thronged around, and cried aloud,
As with one voice, Truth, liberty, and love!
Suddenly fierce confusion fell from heaven
Among them: there was strife, deceit, and fear:
Tyrants rushed in, and did divide the spoil.
This was the shadow of the truth I saw.

text:
Youth, ample, ardent, amorous—youth brimful of grace, of vigor, of enchantment,  
Know you that Age may follow hard upon you with equal grace,  
force, enchantment?
source_text:
Youth, large, lusty, loving--youth full of grace, force, fascination,
Do you know that Old Age may come after you with equal grace,
force, fascination?

text:
I got thy letter, and the birds’;
The maples never knew
That thou wert coming,—I declare,
How red their faces grew!
But, March, forgive me—
And all those hills
Thou leftest me to hue;
There was no purple fitting there,
Thou took’st it all with you.
source_text:
I got your letter, and the birds';
The maples never knew
That you were coming, -- I declare,
How red their faces grew!
But, March, forgive me --
And all those hills
You left for me to hue;
There was no purple suitable,
You took it all with you.

text:
And the wand-like lily, which upreared,
Like a Maenad, its moon-pale cup,
Till the fiery star, which was its eye,
Gazed through the clear dew on the tender sky;
source_text:
And the wand-like lily, which lifted up,
As a Maenad, its moonlight-coloured cup,
Till the fiery star, which is its eye,
Gazed through clear dew on the tender sky;

text:
Each and all, like ministrant angels, were  
To the Sensitive Plant a sweet joy to bear,  
Whilst the lagging hours of day went by  
Like windless clouds across a tender sky.
source_text:
Each and all like ministering angels were
For the Sensitive Plant sweet joy to bear,
Whilst the lagging hours of the day went by
Like windless clouds o’er a tender sky.

text:
When first I gaed to woo my Jenny,
Ye then was trotting wi’ your minnie:
Tho’ ye was wylie, slee, an’ funny,
Ye ne’er was donsie;
But hamely, tawie, quiet, an’ cannie,
An’ unco sonsie.
source_text:
When first I gaed to woo my Jenny,
Ye then was trotting wi’ your minnie:
Tho’ ye was trickie, slee, an’ funnie,
Ye ne’er was donsie;
But hamely, tawie, quiet, an’ cannie,
An’ unco sonsie.

text:
O how shall I, unskilfu’, dare  
The poet’s craft and calling?  
The tunefu’ powers, in happy hours,  
That murmur inspiration;  
Even they maun take a nobler flight  
Than aught they e’er have shown us,  
Ere they recite, in equal strain,  
The charms o’ lovely Davies.
source_text:
O how shall I, unskilfu’, try
The poet’s occupation?
The tunefu’ powers, in happy hours,
That whisper inspiration;
Even they maun dare an effort mair
Than aught they ever gave us,
Ere they rehearse, in equal verse,
The charms o’ lovely Davies.

text:
Even as a bark, which from a rift of mountains,  
Dark, huge, and beetling, on a river  
That there draws in the might of all its fountains,  
Comes forth, while with the speed its timbers quiver,  
Sails, oars, and stream, all bent on one endeavour;  
So, from that rift of light a winged Form  
On all the winds of heaven still drawing ever  
Floated, expanding as it drew: the storm  
Pursued it with fierce blasts, and lightnings swift and warm.
source_text:
Even like a bark, which from a chasm of mountains,
Dark, vast and overhanging, on a river
Which there collects the strength of all its fountains,
Comes forth, whilst with the speed its frame doth quiver,
Sails, oars and stream, tending to one endeavour;
So, from that chasm of light a winged Form
On all the winds of heaven approaching ever
Floated, dilating as it came; the storm
Pursued it with fierce blasts, and lightnings swift and warm.

text:
How the ancient mountains drip with sunset,  
And the brake of dun!  
How the hemlocks are touched with tinsel  
By the wizard sun!
source_text:
How the old mountains drip with sunset,
And the brake of dun!
How the hemlocks are tipped in tinsel
By the wizard sun!

text:
ASIA:
Speak but his name: a world that pines in pain
Craves only this: his name; curses shall drag him down.
source_text:
ASIA:
Utter his name: a world pining in pain
Asks but his name: curses shall drag him down.

text:
’Twas like an eye that seemed to smile on me.  
I gazed, until, by sunmade pallor waned, it sank  
Beneath the billows of the surging sea;  
Yet from its rays my spirit deep love drank,  
And to my brain the boundless world now shrank  
To one thought—one sole image—ay, for ever!  
Even as the dawn, on vapours dimly dank,  
The beams of that one Star did shoot and quiver  
Through my benighted mind—and were extinguished never.
source_text:
‘’Twas like an eye which seemed to smile on me.
I watched, till by the sun made pale, it sank
Under the billows of the heaving sea;
But from its beams deep love my spirit drank,
And to my brain the boundless world now shrank
Into one thought—one image—yes, for ever!
Even like the dayspring, poured on vapours dank,
The beams of that one Star did shoot and quiver
Through my benighted mind—and were extinguished never.

text:
I'll tell you how the sun arose, --
In ribbons, one by one.
The steeples floated in amethyst,
The tidings like squirrels ran.
source_text:
I'll tell you how the sun rose, --
A ribbon at a time.
The steeples swam in amethyst,
The news like squirrels ran.

text:
These brethren, by a thousand signs made plain  
What love Lorenzo bare unto their sister,  
And how she loved him too, each did unchain  
His bitter thoughts to other, well-nigh wilder,  
That he, the servant of their trade’s design,  
Should in their sister’s love be blithe and glad,  
When ’twas their plot, by slow degrees and guile,  
To draw her to some high-born lord and his olive-trees.
source_text:
These brethren having found by many signs
What love Lorenzo for their sister had,
And how she lov'd him too, each unconfines
His bitter thoughts to other, well nigh mad
That he, the servant of their trade designs,
Should in their sister's love be blithe and glad,
When 'twas their plan to coax her by degrees
To some high noble and his olive-trees.

text:
More sights and more adventures, stranger, than thou’dst deem for;
Times once again, now mostly just after sunrise or before sunset,
Sometimes in springtime, oftener in autumn, under weather perfectly clear, in
open view,
Encampments far and nigh, the thronging streets of cities and the shop-windows,
(Explain it as thou wilt—believe it or not—it is all true,
And my comrade there could bear thee witness—we have oft confabbed
thereof,)
Men and scenes, beasts, trees, hues and lines, as plain as plain could be,
Homesteads and home-dooryards, paths bordured with box, lilacs in the corners,
Weddings in churches, thanksgiving feasts, the homecoming of sons long absent,
Gloomy burials, the mother beneath crape-veil and the daughters,
Suits in courts, jury and judge, the accused within the box,
Contenders, battles, multitudes, bridges, wharves,
Now and then faces marked with grief or with gladness,
(I could name them in this very hour, did I behold them once again,)
Reveal’d to me—right there at the sky’s edge,
Or plainly yonder to the left upon the hill-tops.
source_text:
More experiences and sights, stranger, than you’d think for;
Times again, now mostly just after sunrise or before sunset,
Sometimes in spring, oftener in autumn, perfectly clear weather, in
plain sight,
Camps far or near, the crowded streets of cities and the shopfronts,
(Account for it or not--credit or not--it is all true,
And my mate there could tell you the like--we have often confab’d
about it,)
People and scenes, animals, trees, colors and lines, plain as could be,
Farms and dooryards of home, paths border’d with box, lilacs in corners,
Weddings in churches, thanksgiving dinners, returns of long-absent sons,
Glum funerals, the crape-veil’d mother and the daughters,
Trials in courts, jury and judge, the accused in the box,
Contestants, battles, crowds, bridges, wharves,
Now and then mark’d faces of sorrow or joy,
(I could pick them out this moment if I saw them again,)
Show’d to me--just to the right in the sky-edge,
Or plainly there to the left on the hill-tops.

text:
It’s no the keen winter wind,
It’s no the whirling drift and snaw;
But aye the tear wells in my e’e,
To think on him that’s far awa.
source_text:
It’s no the frosty winter wind,
It’s no the driving drift and snaw;
But aye the tear comes in my e’e,
To think on him that’s far awa.

text:
Her message is entrusted
To hands my sight may never see;
For love of her, dear countrymen,
Deem gently, then, of me!
source_text:
Her message is committed
To hands I cannot see;
For love of her, sweet countrymen,
Judge tenderly of me!

text:
And thenceforth shall there be so fast a friendship  
’Twixt thee and me, that neither Fortune,  
The monstrous phantasm that doth dog success,  
That thrifty miser, that unfettered spendthrift,  
Who still, with varying hand, doth interchange  
Ill and good, disgrace and glory; nor shall Time,  
That guiding star of ages, unto whose light  
The winged years hasten through the spaces  
Of their unequal rounds; nor  
Heaven itself, whose fair and lustrous stars  
Do rule the world and lend it ornament, can ever make  
The smallest breach betwixt thee and me,  
Since now I find a haven in thy grace.
source_text:
And thenceforth shall so firm an amity
’Twixt thee and me be, that neither Fortune,
The monstrous phantom which pursues success,
That careful miser, that free prodigal,
Who ever alternates, with changeful hand,
Evil and good, reproach and fame; nor Time,
That lodestar of the ages, to whose beam
The winged years speed o’er the intervals
Of their unequal revolutions; nor
Heaven itself, whose beautiful bright stars
Rule and adorn the world, can ever make
The least division between thee and me,
Since now I find a refuge in thy favour.

text:
It is a far, far treasure to divine,  
And estimate the pearl  
That slipped between my simple fingers then  
When I was but a girl at school!
source_text:
It 's far, far treasure to surmise,
And estimate the pearl
That slipped my simple fingers through
While just a girl at school!

text:
With azure, wavering, stumbling buzz,
Between the light and me;
And then the windows failed, and then
I could not see to see.
source_text:
With blue, uncertain, stumbling buzz,
Between the light and me;
And then the windows failed, and then
I could not see to see.

text:
Auld Neibour,
I’m thrice times doubly o’er your debtor,
For your auld-farrant, kindly letter;
Tho’ I maun say I doubt ye flatter,
Ye speak sae fair;
For my puir, silly, rhyming clatter
Some less maun fare.
source_text:
Auld Neibour,
I’m three times doubly o’er your debtor,
For your auld-farrant, frien’ly letter;
Tho’ I maun say’t I doubt ye flatter,
Ye speak sae fair;
For my puir, silly, rhymin clatter
Some less maun sair.

text:
And thus I tarry here  
Alone, and wanly loitering;  
Though the sedge is withered from the Lake  
And no birds sing. . ..
source_text:
And this is why I sojourn here
Alone and palely loitering;
Though the sedge is withered from the Lake
And no birds sing. . ..

text:
If I have stray’d in those dark paths  
Of life I should have shunn’d,  
As something, loudly, in my breast,  
Remonstrates I have done;
source_text:
If I have wander’d in those paths
Of life I ought to shun,
As something, loudly, in my breast,
Remonstrates I have done;

text:
CHORUS:
O Slavery! thou frost of the world’s first time,
That slayest its blossoms, and leav’st its thorns all bare!
Thy grasp hath marked these limbs with crime,
These brows thy branding garland wear,
Yet the free heart, the steadfast soul
Scorn thy control!
source_text:
CHORUS:
O Slavery! thou frost of the world’s prime,
Killing its flowers and leaving its thorns bare!
Thy touch has stamped these limbs with crime,
These brows thy branding garland bear,
But the free heart, the impassive soul
Scorn thy control!

text:
I must, dearest Agnes, the night is far spent—  
I must roam this evening to Strasburg, intent  
I must seek the sad tomb where my forefathers lie,  
And must delve up their relics from under the stone cold and dry.
source_text:
I must dearest Agnes, the night is far gone—
I must wander this evening to Strasburg alone,
I must seek the drear tomb of my ancestors’ bones,
And must dig their remains from beneath the cold stones.

text:
Coffin that fares through alleys and streets,  
Through day and through night with the great cloud darkening the land,  
With the pomp of the inloop’d flags, with the cities draped in black,  
With the show of the States themselves as of crape-veil’d women standing,  
With processions long and winding and the flambeaus of the night,  
With the countless torches kindled, with the silent sea of faces and the  
uncover’d heads,  
With the waiting depot, the arriving coffin, and the sombre faces,  
With dirges through the night, with the thousand voices mounting strong  
and solemn,  
With all the mournful voices of the dirges pour’d about the coffin,  
The dim-lit churches and the shuddering organs—where amid these  
thou journeyest,  
With the tolling, tolling bells’ perpetual clang,  
Here, coffin that slowest passest,  
I give thee my sprig of lilac.
source_text:
Coffin that passes through lanes and streets,
Through day and night with the great cloud darkening the land,
With the pomp of the inloop’d flags with the cities draped in black,
With the show of the States themselves as of crape-veil’d women standing,
With processions long and winding and the flambeaus of the night,
With the countless torches lit, with the silent sea of faces and the
unbared heads,
With the waiting depot, the arriving coffin, and the sombre faces,
With dirges through the night, with the thousand voices rising strong
and solemn,
With all the mournful voices of the dirges pour’d around the coffin,
The dim-lit churches and the shuddering organs--where amid these
you journey,
With the tolling tolling bells’ perpetual clang,
Here, coffin that slowly passes,
I give you my sprig of lilac.

text:
The homeward-bound and the outward-bound,  
The lovely lost swimmer, the ennuye, the onanist, the maiden that  
loves unrequited, the money-maker,  
The actor and actress, those done with their parts and those  
waiting to begin,  
The loving boy, the husband and wife, the voter, the nominee  
that is chosen and the nominee that hath fail’d,  
The great already known and the great any time after to-day,  
The stammerer, the sick, the perfect-form’d, the homely,  
The criminal that stood in the dock, the judge that sat and sentenced  
him, the ready lawyers, the jury, the audience,  
The laugher and weeper, the dancer, the midnight widow, the red squaw,  
The consumptive, the erysipelatous, the idiot, he that is wrong’d,  
The antipodes, and every one between this and them in the dark,  
I swear they are even’d now—one is no better than the other,  
The night and sleep have made them like and restored them.
source_text:
The homeward bound and the outward bound,
The beautiful lost swimmer, the ennuye, the onanist, the female that
loves unrequited, the money-maker,
The actor and actress, those through with their parts and those
waiting to commence,
The affectionate boy, the husband and wife, the voter, the nominee
that is chosen and the nominee that has fail’d,
The great already known and the great any time after to-day,
The stammerer, the sick, the perfect-form’d, the homely,
The criminal that stood in the box, the judge that sat and sentenced
him, the fluent lawyers, the jury, the audience,
The laugher and weeper, the dancer, the midnight widow, the red squaw,
The consumptive, the erysipalite, the idiot, he that is wrong’d,
The antipodes, and every one between this and them in the dark,
I swear they are averaged now--one is no better than the other,
The night and sleep have liken’d them and restored them.

text:
I would have said—‘Is all here out of tune?’—
But then a voice replied—‘Life!’—I turned, and knew
(O Heaven, show mercy unto such wretchedness!)
source_text:
I would have added—‘is all here amiss?—’
But a voice answered—‘Life!’—I turned, and knew
(O Heaven, have mercy on such wretchedness!)

text:
As Highland cliffs by thunder riven,
When lightnings kindle the stormy heaven,
Are dashed with crashing rattle;
As flames amid a hundred woods,
As headlong foam from hundred floods,
Such is the rage of Battle.
source_text:
As Highland craigs by thunder cleft,
When lightnings fire the stormy lift,
Hurl down with crashing rattle;
As flames among a hundred woods,
As headlong foam from a hundred floods,
Such is the rage of Battle.

text:
And thus, when all the hours had ebbed,
With no abroad-made sound,
Each clasped the other's Crucifix -
No other tie were found -
source_text:
And so, when all the time had leaked,
Without external sound,
Each bound the other's Crucifix -
We gave no other bond -

source_text: “--were not the best way, methinks, albeit it is not to be denied that authorities differ as concerning this point, some contending that the onion is but an unwholesome berry when stricken early from the tree--”

text: “An earl, say ye?” said Dowley; “ye might say more than that and speak no falsehood; for there is no earl in the realm of Bagdemagus that hath an income like unto that. The income of an earl—mf! it is the income of an angel!”
source_text: “An earl, say ye?” said Dowley; “ye could say more than that and speak no lie; there's no earl in the realm of Bagdemagus that hath an income like to that. Income of an earl--mf! it's the income of an angel!”

text:
Chill is the evening blast,  
Of Boreas o'er the pool,  
And dawning is but dreary,  
When birks stand bare at Yule.
source_text:
Cauld is the e’enin blast,
O’ Boreas o’er the pool,
An’ dawin’ it is dreary,
When birks are bare at Yule.
